ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for packaged coconut water beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 standard serving (250 ml carton or bottle)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged coconut water is 250 ml (approximately 250 g), equivalent to one small carton or bottle.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut water, packaged — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged coconut water (CR ID D03040006). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Coconut Water — University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008; Foster-Powell et al., 2002)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 54 for coconut water is consistent with values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for natural coconut water. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (54 × 4.39 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 5.9; the input record states 2.4 which corresponds to a 100 g reference portion GL = (54 × 4.39) / 100 ≈ 2.37, rounded to 2.4. GL per serving retained at 2.4 per 100 g reference basis as supplied by source record.

hypertension.key_factor: Packaged formulation may contain added sugar; sodium 29 mg/100 g is low but added-sugar content in packaged versions is a concern for blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should verify the label for added sugar before consuming packaged coconut water; choose products labelled '100% coconut water, no added sugar' to benefit from the natural potassium (~120 mg/100 g) without the adverse effects of sugar-sweetened beverages on blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 54 (low) but sugar 3.4 g/100 g in liquid form with negligible dietary fibre (0.05 g/100 g) provides minimal glycaemic buffering; packaged versions may contain added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit packaged coconut water due to its liquid sugar content and negligible fibre; if consumed, restrict to small portions (≤150 ml) of products confirmed to contain no added sugar, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.04 g/100 g), low sodium (29 mg/100 g), and natural potassium (~120 mg/100 g) are favourable; however, added sugar in packaged versions may offset cardiovascular benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Packaged coconut water is low in fat and sodium and provides natural potassium, which supports cardiovascular health, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose unsweetened varieties and limit intake to avoid excess sugar consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged coconut water is a ready-to-drink beverage derived from the clear liquid found inside young green coconuts (Cocos nucifera). It is commercially processed, pasteurised, and sold in cartons, bottles, or cans. The liquid is naturally light, slightly sweet, and mildly nutty in flavour with a thin, watery texture. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 18 kcal, 4.39 g carbohydrate (of which 3.4 g is sugar), 0.07 g protein, 0.04 g fat, 0.05 g dietary fibre, 29 mg sodium, 10 mg calcium, and an estimated 120 mg potassium. The glycemic index is approximately 54 (low), yielding a glycemic load of about 2.4 per 100 g reference portion. Coconut water is widely consumed across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and tropical regions as a natural hydration beverage. In Singapore and Malaysia it is commonly sold in supermarkets and convenience stores under brands such as Marigold, Pocari, and Vita Coco. Clinically, packaged coconut water is a low-calorie, low-fat beverage with moderate natural sugar content. Its potassium content is beneficial for blood pressure regulation; however, the packaged form may contain added sugar, which is a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Consumers are advised to check labels for added sugar and choose 100% pure coconut water products. It is suitable for plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets.
common_user_queries: Is coconut water good for diabetes?
How many calories in coconut water?
Is coconut water high in sugar?
Can I drink coconut water if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of coconut water?
Is bottled coconut water the same as fresh coconut water?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D03040006) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 54 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database literature for coconut water. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and supported.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; coconut water contains negligible saturated fat). iron_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based beverage contains no cholesterol). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this packaged coconut water record. potassium_mg estimated at 120 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central reference for coconut water (FDC ID 1101226) as HPB record does not report potassium explicitly. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input record has been removed as coconut water at 4.39 g carbohydrate/100 g is borderline for strict ketogenic diets and the tag is not clinically defensible for a standard 250 ml serving (~11 g carbohydrate). GI glycemic load of 2.4 is retained as the per-100 g reference basis value from the input source record.
